title: About Islam.wiki
content:
Islam.wiki is the most comprehensive Islamic knowledge base on the internet. We are a community-driven, scholar-verified reference covering all aspects of Islam: Quran, Hadith, Seerah, classical scholarship, and encyclopedic articles.

Our Mission

To make authentic Islamic knowledge freely accessible to everyone, in every language. We follow the methodology of Ahl us-Sunnah wal-Jama'ah, presenting all four schools of jurisprudence equally and drawing from classical scholarship as our primary sources.

Part of the Ummat Ecosystem

Islam.wiki is one of five apps in the Ummat ecosystem, built to serve the global Muslim community.

title: How to Contribute
content:
Islam.wiki is a community-driven wiki. Anyone can contribute by creating an account and submitting edits.

Trust Levels

All new accounts start at Trust Level 0. Your edits will be reviewed by AI and human moderators. As your contributions are approved, your trust level increases automatically.

  • Level 0 (New): All edits require review
  • Level 1 (Trusted): Minor edits auto-approved
  • Level 2 (Editor): Most edits auto-approved, can review others
  • Level 3 (Moderator): Full editing privileges, can ban
  • Level 4 (Admin): Full site access

Editorial Guidelines

All content must be sourced from primary Islamic references. Every hadith must include its collection, book, and number. Every scholarly opinion must cite the scholar and their work. We do not publish personal opinions or unsourced claims.

title: Content Guidelines
content:
Islam.wiki follows strict editorial and theological guidelines to maintain accuracy and trustworthiness.

Sources

We accept content sourced from: Quran, authentic Hadith collections, classical scholarly works, and reputable modern scholarship. We do not accept content from unknown or unreliable sources.

Theological Framework

We follow the methodology of Ahl us-Sunnah wal-Jama'ah. This means: all four madhabs (Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, Hanbali) are presented equally. In matters of aqeedah, we primarily follow the Athari creed while accepting Ash'ari and Maturidi as legitimate positions within Sunni Islam.

title: Contact
content:
Islam.wiki is developed by Ummat Dev.

For questions, corrections, or partnership inquiries, reach out through our contact form or email.

title: Islamic Glossary
content:
A comprehensive glossary of commonly used Islamic terms and their meanings.

A

Adhan (أذان)
The Islamic call to prayer, announced five times daily from the mosque.
Ahl us-Sunnah (أهل السنة)
The People of the Sunnah. The mainstream body of Muslims who follow the Quran, the Sunnah of the Prophet, and the consensus of the Companions.
Akhirah (آخرة)
The Hereafter. The afterlife including the Day of Judgment, Paradise, and Hellfire.
Aqeedah (عقيدة)
Islamic creed or theology. The fundamental beliefs of a Muslim.

B

Barzakh (برزخ)
The barrier between the worldly life and the Hereafter. The period between death and resurrection.
Bid'ah (بدعة)
Innovation in religious matters. Something introduced into the religion that has no basis in the Quran or Sunnah.
Bismillah (بسم الله)
"In the name of Allah." Said before beginning any action.

D-Z

Da'wah (دعوة)
Invitation to Islam.
Dhikr (ذكر)
Remembrance of Allah through words of praise.
Fatwa (فتوى)
A religious ruling issued by a qualified scholar.
Fiqh (فقه)
Islamic jurisprudence.
Hadith (حديث)
A narration reporting the words, actions, or approvals of the Prophet Muhammad.
Halal (حلال)
Permissible according to Islamic law.
Haram (حرام)
Forbidden according to Islamic law.
Ihsan (إحسان)
Excellence in worship.
Iman (إيمان)
Faith.
Jannah (جنة)
Paradise.
Madhab (مذهب)
School of Islamic jurisprudence.
Qadr (قدر)
Divine decree.
Sahih (صحيح)
Authentic (hadith classification).
Sunnah (سنة)
The way of the Prophet Muhammad.
Tawhid (توحيد)
The Oneness of Allah.
Ummah (أمة)
The global Muslim community.
Zakat (زكاة)
Obligatory charity.

title: Islamic Timeline
content:
A chronological overview of major events in Islamic history from the birth of Prophet Muhammad to the modern era.

Pre-Islamic Arabia (Before 610 CE)

The Arabian Peninsula before Islam was characterized by tribal societies, polytheistic worship around the Ka'bah, and the influence of both the Byzantine and Sassanid empires.

Prophetic Period (610-632 CE)

  • 610: First revelation in Cave Hira
  • 622: Hijrah to Madinah
  • 624: Battle of Badr
  • 630: Conquest of Makkah
  • 632: Death of the Prophet

Rashidun Caliphate (632-661 CE)

The four rightly-guided caliphs: Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ali.

Later Periods

  • 661-750: Umayyad Caliphate
  • 750-1258: Abbasid Caliphate (Islamic Golden Age)
  • 1299-1922: Ottoman Empire

title: The Five Pillars of Islam
content:
The Five Pillars of Islam (Arkan al-Islam) are the five fundamental acts of worship that form the foundation of a Muslim's life.

1. Shahada

The testimony that there is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah.

2. Salah

Five daily prayers: Fajr, Dhuhr, Asr, Maghrib, and Isha.

3. Zakat

Obligatory charity of 2.5% of savings annually.

4. Sawm

Fasting from dawn to sunset during Ramadan.

5. Hajj

Pilgrimage to Makkah at least once in a lifetime for those able.

title: The Six Pillars of Iman (Faith)
content:
The Six Pillars of Iman are the core beliefs every Muslim must hold, from the Hadith of Jibril.

1. Belief in Allah

His existence, oneness, names and attributes.

2. Belief in the Angels

Created from light, they carry out Allah's commands.

3. Belief in the Revealed Books

Torah, Psalms, Gospel, and the Quran (final and preserved).

4. Belief in the Prophets

All prophets from Adam to Muhammad.

5. Belief in the Day of Judgment

Resurrection, reckoning, Paradise and Hellfire.

6. Belief in Divine Decree (Qadr)

Allah's knowledge, recording, will, and creation of all events.

title: How to Pray in Islam
content:
Salah is the second pillar of Islam. Muslims pray five times daily at prescribed times.

Prerequisites

  • Wudu (ablution)
  • Clean body, clothes, and prayer area
  • Facing the Qiblah
  • Proper intention

The Five Prayers

  • Fajr: 2 rak'ahs, before sunrise
  • Dhuhr: 4 rak'ahs, after midday
  • Asr: 4 rak'ahs, late afternoon
  • Maghrib: 3 rak'ahs, after sunset
  • Isha: 4 rak'ahs, night

title: Guide to Ramadan
content:
Ramadan is the ninth month of the Islamic calendar and the month of fasting.

Fasting

Muslims fast from dawn to sunset, abstaining from food, drink, and marital relations.

Spiritual Practices

  • Tarawih (night prayers)
  • Quran recitation
  • I'tikaf (seclusion in the last 10 days)
  • Laylat al-Qadr (Night of Power)

Eid al-Fitr

Ramadan concludes with Eid al-Fitr, a day of celebration and charity.

title: Guide to Hajj
content:
Hajj is the fifth pillar of Islam, the annual pilgrimage to Makkah during 8-12 Dhul Hijjah.

Key Rituals

  1. Ihram at the miqat
  2. Tawaf al-Qudum (7 circuits of the Ka'bah)
  3. Sa'i between Safa and Marwah
  4. Standing at Arafat (9th Dhul Hijjah)
  5. Muzdalifah overnight
  6. Stoning of Jamarat
  7. Animal sacrifice
  8. Tawaf al-Ifadah
  9. Farewell Tawaf

title: The Quran: An Overview
content:
The Quran is the central text of Islam, the literal word of Allah revealed to Prophet Muhammad over 23 years.

Structure

114 surahs, approximately 6,236 ayahs, divided into 30 juz' for ease of recitation.

Preservation

Preserved through memorization and writing, compiled under Abu Bakr and standardized under Uthman. It remains unchanged today.

Sciences

Tafsir (exegesis), Asbab an-Nuzul (circumstances of revelation), Tajwid (recitation rules), and Qira'at (variant readings).

title: Hadith Sciences: An Overview
content:
Hadith refers to the recorded sayings, actions, and approvals of the Prophet Muhammad.

Components

  • Isnad: Chain of narration
  • Matn: Text of the narration

Classification

  • Sahih: Authentic
  • Hasan: Good
  • Da'if: Weak
  • Mawdu': Fabricated

Major Collections

Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, Tirmidhi, Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, Muwatta Malik, and Musnad Ahmad.

title: The Islamic Calendar
content:
The Islamic (Hijri) calendar is a lunar calendar of 12 months in a year of 354-355 days.

The Twelve Months

  1. Muharram (sacred)
  2. Safar
  3. Rabi al-Awwal
  4. Rabi al-Thani
  5. Jumada al-Ula
  6. Jumada al-Thani
  7. Rajab (sacred)
  8. Sha'ban
  9. Ramadan
  10. Shawwal
  11. Dhul Qa'dah (sacred)
  12. Dhul Hijjah (sacred)

title: Prophets in Islam
content:
Muslims believe in all prophets sent by Allah, from Adam to Muhammad. 25 are named in the Quran.

Ulul-Azm (Prophets of Strong Will)

Nuh, Ibrahim, Musa, Isa, and Muhammad are the five prophets of resolute determination.

Key Beliefs

  • All came with the same message: worship Allah alone
  • Prophets were human, not divine
  • Muhammad is the final prophet (Quran 33:40)

title: Makkah (Mecca)
content:
Makkah is the holiest city in Islam, birthplace of Prophet Muhammad and home to the Ka'bah.

The Ka'bah

Originally built by Ibrahim and Ismail, it is the qiblah for Muslims worldwide.

Hajj and Umrah

Millions visit annually for pilgrimage.

title: Madinah (Medina)
content:
Madinah is the second holiest city, the destination of the Hijrah and home of the Prophet's Mosque.

Al-Masjid an-Nabawi

Built by the Prophet himself, it contains his burial place.

title: Jerusalem (Al-Quds) in Islam
content:
Jerusalem is the third holiest city, home to Al-Aqsa Mosque and associated with the Night Journey.

Al-Masjid al-Aqsa

Referenced in Quran 17:1. The Prophet led all prophets in prayer here.

First Qiblah

Muslims prayed toward Jerusalem for about 16 months before the qiblah changed to Makkah.

title: Tawhid — The Oneness of Allah
content:
Tawhid is the most fundamental concept in Islam: the absolute oneness of Allah.

Three Categories

  • Tawhid ar-Rububiyyah: Oneness of Lordship. Allah alone is the Creator, Sustainer, and Disposer of all affairs.
  • Tawhid al-Uluhiyyah: Oneness of Worship. All worship must be directed to Allah alone.
  • Tawhid al-Asma wa's-Sifat: Oneness of Names and Attributes. Allah's names and attributes are unique and cannot be compared to creation.

Evidence

"Say: He is Allah, the One. Allah, the Eternal Refuge. He neither begets nor is born. Nor is there to Him any equivalent." (Quran 112:1-4)

title: The Companions of the Prophet (Sahabah)
content:
The Sahabah are the companions of Prophet Muhammad who saw him, believed in him, and died upon Islam. They hold the highest status in the Muslim community after the prophets.

The Ten Promised Paradise

Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, Talhah, Zubayr, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, Sa'id ibn Zayd, Abdur-Rahman ibn Awf, and Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah.

Ahl us-Sunnah View

We love and respect all the Companions, refrain from speaking ill of any of them, and believe that their disputes were based on ijtihad (independent reasoning).

title: Mothers of the Believers (Ummahat al-Mu'minin)
content:
The Mothers of the Believers are the wives of Prophet Muhammad. The Quran gives them a special status and title (Quran 33:6).

The Wives

  • Khadijah bint Khuwailid: First wife, first Muslim, mother of most of his children
  • Sawda bint Zam'ah
  • Aisha bint Abu Bakr: Narrator of over 2,000 hadiths, great scholar
  • Hafsah bint Umar: Guardian of the first Quran manuscript
  • Zaynab bint Khuzaymah: Known as 'Mother of the Poor'
  • Umm Salamah: Known for wisdom and counsel
  • Zaynab bint Jahsh
  • Juwayriyyah bint al-Harith
  • Safiyyah bint Huyayy
  • Umm Habibah
  • Maymunah bint al-Harith

title: Ahl al-Bayt — The Prophet's Family
content:
Ahl al-Bayt (People of the House) refers to the family of Prophet Muhammad. Muslims are commanded to love and honor them.

Who Are Ahl al-Bayt?

The Prophet's wives, his daughter Fatimah, Ali ibn Abi Talib, Hasan, and Husayn. The broader definition includes all of Banu Hashim.

Ahl us-Sunnah Position

We love Ahl al-Bayt as the Quran commands (42:23), without exaggeration or deficiency. We do not worship them nor attribute divine qualities to them, and we love the Companions alongside them.

title: Shariah — Islamic Law
content:
Shariah (the way or path) refers to the totality of Islamic guidance derived from the Quran and Sunnah. It covers worship, personal conduct, family law, commerce, governance, and more.

Sources of Shariah

  1. Quran: The primary source
  2. Sunnah: The Prophet's example
  3. Ijma: Scholarly consensus
  4. Qiyas: Analogical reasoning

Five Categories of Actions

  • Wajib/Fard: Obligatory
  • Mustahabb: Recommended
  • Mubah: Permissible
  • Makruh: Disliked
  • Haram: Forbidden

title: The Hanafi Madhab
content:
The Hanafi school is the oldest and most widely followed madhab, founded by Imam Abu Hanifah (d. 767 CE) in Kufa, Iraq.

Methodology

Known for extensive use of ra'y (legal reasoning) and qiyas (analogy). Prioritizes the Quran, then Sunnah, then consensus, then individual companion opinions, then analogy.

Geographic Spread

Dominant in Turkey, Central Asia, South Asia, the Balkans, and parts of the Arab world. It is the most followed madhab globally.

Key Students

Abu Yusuf and Muhammad al-Shaybani, whose works form the basis of Hanafi jurisprudence.

title: The Maliki Madhab
content:
The Maliki school was founded by Imam Malik ibn Anas (d. 795 CE) in Medina.

Methodology

Known for heavy reliance on the practice of the people of Medina (Amal Ahl al-Madinah) as a source of law, in addition to Quran, Sunnah, and consensus. Also uses istihsan (juristic preference) and maslahah (public interest).

Geographic Spread

Dominant in North Africa, West Africa, parts of the Gulf, and historically in Muslim Spain (al-Andalus).

Key Works

Al-Muwatta by Imam Malik is both a hadith collection and a fiqh manual.

title: The Shafi'i Madhab
content:
The Shafi'i school was founded by Imam Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i (d. 820 CE), who studied under both Imam Malik and the students of Abu Hanifah.

Methodology

Al-Shafi'i formalized usul al-fiqh (principles of jurisprudence) in his landmark work al-Risalah. His methodology systematically prioritizes Quran, Sunnah, ijma, then qiyas, with strict rules for each.

Geographic Spread

Dominant in East Africa, Southeast Asia, Egypt, Yemen, and parts of the Levant.

title: The Hanbali Madhab
content:
The Hanbali school was founded by Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal (d. 855 CE), the great muhaddith (hadith scholar) of Baghdad.

Methodology

Known for the strongest adherence to hadith among the four madhabs. Prefers a weak hadith over qiyas. Minimal use of ra'y (opinion). Known for its textualist approach.

Geographic Spread

Historically centered in Iraq and Syria. Today dominant in Saudi Arabia and parts of the Gulf.

Legacy

The Hanbali tradition influenced later scholars like Ibn Taymiyyah and Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab.

title: Islamic Ethics (Akhlaq)
content:
Akhlaq (ethics, character) is central to Islam. The Prophet said: 'I was sent to perfect good character' (Musnad Ahmad).

Foundational Virtues

  • Sidq (Truthfulness)
  • Amanah (Trustworthiness)
  • Sabr (Patience)
  • Shukr (Gratitude)
  • Tawadu (Humility)
  • Adl (Justice)
  • Ihsan (Excellence)
  • Rahmah (Mercy)

Prohibited Traits

  • Lying, backbiting, slander
  • Arrogance and envy
  • Miserliness and extravagance

title: Sufism (Tasawwuf) in Islam
content:
Tasawwuf is the science of purifying the heart and attaining the station of ihsan (excellence in worship). It has been part of mainstream Sunni Islam since the earliest generations.

Core Concepts

  • Tazkiyah: Purification of the self
  • Muraqabah: Watchfulness of Allah
  • Dhikr: Remembrance of Allah
  • Maqamat: Spiritual stations (tawbah, sabr, zuhd, tawakkul, rida)

Major Tariqahs

Qadiriyyah, Naqshbandiyyah, Shadhiliyyah, Chishtiyyah, Tijaniyyah.

Ahl us-Sunnah Position

Tasawwuf that adheres to the Quran and Sunnah is accepted. Extremism, innovation, and practices contradicting the Shariah are rejected.

title: Islamic Education System
content:
Education has been central to Islam since the first word revealed: 'Read' (Iqra). The Prophet established the first school in Masjid al-Nabawi.

Historical Institutions

  • Kuttab: Elementary Quran schools
  • Halaqah: Study circles in mosques
  • Madrasah: Formal institutions of learning (from the 11th century)
  • Jami'ah: Universities (al-Qarawiyyin, al-Azhar)

Traditional Curriculum

Quran, Hadith, Fiqh, Arabic grammar, Tafsir, Aqeedah, Logic, and Rhetoric.

title: Complete Guide to Zakat
content:
Zakat is the third pillar of Islam, an obligatory annual payment of 2.5% on wealth that exceeds the nisab (minimum threshold).

Conditions

  • Muslim, free, adult
  • Wealth above nisab for one lunar year
  • Nisab: 85g gold or 595g silver equivalent

Eight Categories of Recipients

  1. The poor (fuqara)
  2. The needy (masakin)
  3. Zakat collectors
  4. Those whose hearts are to be reconciled
  5. Freeing slaves
  6. Those in debt
  7. In the cause of Allah
  8. The stranded traveler

title: Guide to Islamic Marriage (Nikah)
content:
Marriage in Islam is a sacred contract (aqd) between a man and a woman, described by the Prophet as half of one's religion.

Conditions

  • Consent of both parties
  • Wali (guardian) for the bride
  • Two witnesses
  • Mahr (dowry) from groom to bride
  • Ijab and qabul (offer and acceptance)

Rights and Duties

Both spouses have mutual rights: kind treatment, maintenance, intimacy, and respect. The husband provides financially; the wife manages the household by mutual agreement.

title: Death and the Afterlife in Islam
content:
Islam provides a comprehensive framework for understanding death, the intermediate realm, resurrection, and the final abode.

The Journey

  1. Death: The angel of death takes the soul
  2. Barzakh: The intermediate realm. Questioning by Munkar and Nakir
  3. Resurrection (Ba'th): All souls are raised
  4. Reckoning (Hisab): Every deed is accounted for
  5. The Scale (Mizan): Deeds are weighed
  6. The Bridge (Sirat): Crossing over Hellfire
  7. Final Abode: Jannah (Paradise) or Jahannam (Hellfire)

title: Islamic Greetings and Etiquette
content:
The Islamic greeting 'As-Salamu Alaykum' (peace be upon you) is both a greeting and a supplication.

The Sunnah of Greeting

  • The one who initiates the greeting earns more reward
  • The rider greets the walker; the walker greets the one sitting; the small group greets the larger
  • Response: 'Wa Alaykum as-Salam wa Rahmatullahi wa Barakatuh'

Other Islamic Expressions

  • Bismillah: In the name of Allah (before actions)
  • Alhamdulillah: All praise is for Allah
  • SubhanAllah: Glory be to Allah
  • InshaAllah: If Allah wills (for future plans)
  • MashaAllah: As Allah has willed (expressing admiration)
  • Jazakallahu Khayran: May Allah reward you with good

title: Significance of Islamic Months
content:
Each month in the Islamic calendar carries its own significance and recommended acts of worship.

Key Months

  • Muharram: Day of Ashura (10th) - fasting recommended
  • Rabi al-Awwal: The Prophet's birth month
  • Rajab: Month of the Isra and Mi'raj
  • Sha'ban: The Prophet would fast most of this month
  • Ramadan: Obligatory fasting, Laylat al-Qadr
  • Shawwal: Eid al-Fitr, recommended 6 days of fasting
  • Dhul Hijjah: First 10 days are the best days, Day of Arafah, Eid al-Adha, Hajj

title: The Ka'bah
content:
The Ka'bah is the cube-shaped structure at the center of Masjid al-Haram in Makkah. It is the qiblah (direction of prayer) for all Muslims and the focal point of Hajj and Umrah.

History

According to Islamic tradition, the Ka'bah was originally built by Prophet Ibrahim and his son Ismail. Before Islam, it housed 360 idols which were removed by the Prophet Muhammad after the conquest of Makkah.

The Black Stone (al-Hajar al-Aswad)

Set in the eastern corner of the Ka'bah, it is believed to have been sent down from Paradise. Pilgrims begin their tawaf at this point.

The Kiswah

The black cloth covering the Ka'bah is replaced annually. It is embroidered with gold Quranic calligraphy.

title: Masjid al-Haram (The Sacred Mosque)
content:
Masjid al-Haram in Makkah is the largest and holiest mosque in Islam. It surrounds the Ka'bah and can accommodate over 2 million worshippers.

Virtue

Prayer in Masjid al-Haram is equivalent to 100,000 prayers elsewhere (Ibn Majah).

Key Features

  • The Ka'bah
  • The Station of Ibrahim (Maqam Ibrahim)
  • The Well of Zamzam
  • Safa and Marwah (for Sa'i)
  • The Hatim (Hijr Ismail)

title: Masjid al-Aqsa
content:
Masjid al-Aqsa is the third holiest mosque in Islam, located in Jerusalem. It is the entire compound on the Temple Mount (Haram ash-Sharif), not just the building with the silver dome.

Virtue

Prayer in Masjid al-Aqsa is equivalent to 500 prayers elsewhere. The Prophet led all prophets in prayer here during the Night Journey.

Historical Significance

It was the first qiblah. Umar ibn al-Khattab personally accepted Jerusalem's surrender in 637 CE and prayed at the site.

title: Women Scholars in Islamic History
content:
Women have played a crucial role in Islamic scholarship from the earliest generations. Aisha bint Abu Bakr alone narrated over 2,000 hadiths and was consulted by the greatest scholars.

Early Women Scholars

  • Aisha bint Abu Bakr: Greatest female scholar of Islam
  • Fatimah bint Muhammad: The Prophet's daughter
  • Hafsa bint Umar: Guardian of the Quran manuscript
  • Umm Salamah: Major narrator and advisor

Later Scholars

  • Fatima al-Fihri: Founded the University of al-Qarawiyyin (859 CE)
  • Shuhda al-Katiba: 12th-century hadith scholar
  • Zaynab bint al-Kamal: Taught hadith in Damascus

title: Contributions of Islamic Civilization
content:
Muslim scholars made groundbreaking contributions to science, medicine, mathematics, and philosophy that shaped the modern world.

Mathematics

Al-Khwarizmi developed algebra and algorithms. The Hindu-Arabic numeral system was transmitted to Europe through Muslim scholars.

Medicine

Ibn Sina's Canon of Medicine was used in European universities for centuries. Al-Zahrawi is considered the father of modern surgery.

Optics

Ibn al-Haytham (Alhazen) is considered the father of modern optics and the scientific method.

Geography

Al-Idrisi created the most accurate world map of his time. Ibn Battuta traveled more extensively than Marco Polo.

title: Dawah Methodology
content:
Dawah (inviting to Islam) is an obligation on every Muslim according to their ability. The Quran says: 'Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good instruction' (16:125).

Principles

  • Wisdom and beautiful preaching
  • Knowledge before action
  • Gentleness and patience
  • Leading by example
  • Addressing the heart, not just the mind

The Prophet's Method

He began with tawhid, used kindness, respected people's intelligence, and never forced anyone. 'There is no compulsion in religion' (Quran 2:256).

title: Islamic Art and Architecture
content:
Islamic art is characterized by geometric patterns, calligraphy, arabesque designs, and the avoidance of depicting living beings in religious contexts.

Calligraphy

Arabic calligraphy is the highest art form in Islamic civilization. Major scripts include Naskh, Thuluth, Diwani, and Kufic.

Architecture

  • Mosques: Dome of the Rock, Great Mosque of Cordoba, Sultan Ahmed Mosque
  • Palaces: Alhambra, Topkapi
  • Madrasas: Al-Azhar, Registan

Geometric Patterns

Islamic geometric art reflects the infinite nature of Allah. Patterns based on circles, squares, and stars can tile infinitely without repetition.

title: New Muslim's Guide
content:
Congratulations on accepting Islam. Here are the first steps in your new journey of faith.

Immediate Steps

  1. Learn the Shahada in Arabic
  2. Learn how to perform wudu (ablution)
  3. Learn the five daily prayers (start with the basics)
  4. Find a local mosque or Islamic center

First Month

  • Read the Quran in your language
  • Learn the short surahs for prayer
  • Connect with other Muslims
  • Be patient with yourself

Common Questions

Do I need to change my name? Not required, but many choose an Islamic name. Do I need to be circumcised? Scholars differ; it is sunnah but some say obligatory. What about my non-Muslim family? Maintain the best relations with them.

title: Guide to Islamic Finance
content:
Islamic finance operates on the principle that money itself has no intrinsic value and must be tied to real economic activity. Interest (riba) is strictly prohibited.

Key Principles

  • No riba (interest)
  • No gharar (excessive uncertainty)
  • No maysir (gambling)
  • Asset-backed transactions
  • Profit and loss sharing

Islamic Financial Instruments

  • Murabaha: Cost-plus financing
  • Ijara: Leasing
  • Musharakah: Joint venture
  • Mudarabah: Silent partnership
  • Sukuk: Islamic bonds
  • Takaful: Islamic insurance

title: Islamic Sects and Groups
content:
The Muslim ummah, while united in core beliefs, has various theological and jurisprudential groups. Islam.wiki presents the Ahl us-Sunnah wal-Jama'ah perspective.

Major Groups

  • Ahl us-Sunnah: The majority, following the Quran, Sunnah, and way of the Companions
  • Shi'ah: Believe in the imamate of Ali and his descendants
  • Ibadiyyah: Moderate Kharijite-origin group, mainly in Oman

Ahl us-Sunnah Theological Schools

  • Athariyyah: Textualist, following the Salaf
  • Ash'ariyyah: Rational theology of al-Ash'ari
  • Maturidiyyah: Rational theology of al-Maturidi

title: Environmental Ethics in Islam
content:
Islam considers humans as stewards (khulafa) of the earth. Environmental responsibility is a religious duty.

Quranic Principles

  • 'It is He who has made you stewards of the earth' (6:165)
  • 'Do not cause corruption on the earth after it has been set in order' (7:56)
  • 'There is no creature on earth nor bird that flies with its wings but they are communities like you' (6:38)

Prophetic Guidance

The Prophet prohibited wasting water even when performing ablution at a flowing river. He established protected areas (hima) for wildlife. He planted trees and encouraged others to do so.

title: Quran Memorization (Hifz)
content:
Memorizing the entire Quran (hifz) is a noble tradition that dates back to the Prophet's time. A person who memorizes the Quran is called a hafiz.

Virtue

'The one who is proficient in the Quran will be with the noble righteous scribes (angels)' (Bukhari and Muslim).

Methods

  • Repetition: Read each page 20-30 times until memorized
  • Consistency: Daily practice, even a small amount
  • Review (Muraja'ah): Regular revision of memorized portions
  • Teacher: Study under a qualified teacher for proper tajwid

Tips

Best time for memorization is after Fajr. Link new memorization with existing portions. Use one mushaf consistently.

title: Mosque Etiquette (Adab al-Masjid)
content:
The mosque is the house of Allah and has specific etiquettes (adab) that Muslims should observe.

Entering the Mosque

  • Enter with the right foot, saying the dua of entering
  • Pray two rak'ahs (Tahiyyat al-Masjid) before sitting
  • Sit quietly and engage in dhikr or Quran

General Etiquettes

  • Maintain cleanliness and avoid strong odors
  • Do not step over worshippers
  • Keep phones silent
  • Do not buy or sell in the mosque
  • Straighten the rows for congregational prayer

title: Islamic Psychology (Ilm an-Nafs)
content:
Islamic psychology addresses the human soul (nafs), its diseases, and purification. Muslim scholars wrote extensively on the inner life centuries before modern psychology.

Three States of the Nafs

  • Nafs al-Ammarah: The commanding self that inclines toward evil (12:53)
  • Nafs al-Lawwamah: The self-reproaching self that recognizes its faults (75:2)
  • Nafs al-Mutma'innah: The tranquil self at peace with Allah (89:27)

Diseases of the Heart

Envy (hasad), arrogance (kibr), showing off (riya), love of the world (hubb ad-dunya), and anger (ghadab).

title: Islamic Etiquette of Food and Drink
content:
Islam provides comprehensive guidance on eating and drinking, covering what to eat, how to eat, and manners during meals.

Before Eating

  • Say Bismillah (In the name of Allah)
  • Wash hands
  • Eat with the right hand

During the Meal

  • Eat from what is nearest to you
  • Do not criticize food
  • Do not eat while reclining
  • Eat moderately: 'A third for food, a third for drink, a third for breath' (Tirmidhi)

After Eating

Say 'Alhamdulillah' (All praise is for Allah). The Prophet would lick his fingers and clean the plate.

title: Taqwa — God-Consciousness
content:
Definition

Taqwa (تقوى) is one of the most important concepts in Islam, often translated as God-consciousness, piety, or mindfulness of Allah. Linguistically it comes from the root wiqayah, meaning to protect or shield oneself. In Islamic usage, taqwa means placing a barrier between yourself and the displeasure of Allah by obeying His commands and avoiding His prohibitions.

Taqwa in the Quran

The word taqwa and its derivatives appear over 250 times in the Quran, making it one of the most frequently referenced concepts. Allah says: 'O you who believe, have taqwa of Allah as He deserves to be feared, and do not die except as Muslims' (3:102). The Quran also states: 'The most honored of you in the sight of Allah is the one with the most taqwa' (49:13), establishing taqwa as the true measure of a person's worth.

How Taqwa Is Developed

Scholars describe several paths to developing taqwa. Regular prayer and worship build awareness of Allah throughout the day. Fasting trains the soul to resist desires. Studying the Quran and reflecting on its meanings deepens understanding of what Allah requires. Keeping company with righteous people reinforces good habits. Self-accounting (muhasabah) at the end of each day helps identify areas for improvement.

Signs of Taqwa

Ali ibn Abi Talib described the person of taqwa as one who is humble in demeanor, generous in giving, careful in speech, and consistent in worship. Such a person avoids doubtful matters, is grateful in ease and patient in hardship, and fears standing before Allah on the Day of Judgment.

title: Ihsan — Excellence in Worship
content:
Definition

Ihsan (إحسان) means excellence, perfection, or doing something in the most beautiful manner. In Islamic theology, it refers to the highest level of faith, beyond Islam (submission) and Iman (belief). The Prophet Muhammad defined it in the famous Hadith of Jibril: 'It is to worship Allah as if you see Him, and if you cannot see Him, then know that He sees you' (Bukhari and Muslim).

The Three Levels of Religion

The Hadith of Jibril establishes three levels: Islam (the five pillars of practice), Iman (the six pillars of belief), and Ihsan (the perfection of both). While Islam and Iman deal with outward actions and inward beliefs respectively, Ihsan addresses the quality and sincerity behind both. A person may pray (Islam) and believe in prayer's obligation (Iman), but performing that prayer with full presence of heart, focus, and awareness of Allah is Ihsan.

Ihsan in Daily Life

Ihsan extends beyond worship to every aspect of life. The Prophet said: 'Allah has prescribed ihsan in everything. When you slaughter, slaughter well. Let each of you sharpen his blade and spare suffering to the animal he slaughters' (Muslim). This hadith shows that ihsan applies to how we treat animals, conduct business, interact with family, and fulfill professional responsibilities. It is the pursuit of excellence as a form of worship.

Relationship to Tasawwuf

Classical scholars identify ihsan as the domain of tasawwuf (Islamic spirituality). While fiqh governs the outward form of actions and aqeedah governs beliefs, tasawwuf focuses on perfecting the inner state that produces ihsan. The great scholars of the past, including Imam al-Ghazali, Ibn al-Qayyim, and al-Muhasibi, wrote extensively on how to attain this station.

title: Tawakkul — Reliance on Allah
content:
Definition

Tawakkul (توكل) means placing complete trust and reliance on Allah while taking all necessary practical measures. It is not passive resignation but rather an active trust: you tie your camel and then place your trust in Allah, as the Prophet taught. Tawakkul is the heart's total dependence on Allah as the sole Arranger of all affairs.

Quranic Foundation

Allah says: 'And whoever places their trust in Allah, He is sufficient for them' (65:3). The Quran also states: 'So upon Allah let the believers rely' (3:122). These verses establish tawakkul as a defining quality of true believers. The Quran mentions tawakkul in over 70 places, consistently linking it to faith and courage.

Tawakkul vs. Tawakul

Scholars carefully distinguish between tawakkul (trust with effort) and tawakul (lazy passivity). Umar ibn al-Khattab said: 'If you truly relied on Allah as He should be relied upon, He would provide for you as He provides for the birds: they go out hungry in the morning and return full in the evening' (Tirmidhi). Notice that even the birds go out and seek their provision. Tawakkul means doing your part fully while recognizing that outcomes belong to Allah alone.

Practical Application

A student with tawakkul studies diligently for exams while trusting Allah with the results. A sick person seeks medical treatment while knowing that only Allah grants healing. A business owner takes all reasonable precautions while accepting that profit and loss are in Allah's hands. The Prophet himself, the greatest example of tawakkul, wore armor in battle, planned strategies, and took practical measures in every situation.

title: Sabr — Patience and Perseverance
content:
Definition

Sabr (صبر) encompasses patience, perseverance, steadfastness, and endurance. It is one of the most frequently praised qualities in the Quran, mentioned over 90 times. Sabr means restraining the soul from anxiety, the tongue from complaining, and the limbs from sinful actions during times of difficulty.

Three Types of Sabr

Scholars identify three categories of patience. First, sabr in obeying Allah: persisting in worship even when it is difficult, praying Fajr on cold mornings, fasting long summer days, and giving charity when money is tight. Second, sabr in avoiding sin: resisting temptation, controlling anger, and walking away from haram even when it is available and appealing. Third, sabr during trials: enduring illness, poverty, loss, and hardship without losing faith or blaming Allah.

Rewards of Sabr

Allah says: 'Indeed, the patient will be given their reward without measure' (39:10). This is one of the only deeds for which the reward is described as unlimited. The Prophet said: 'No fatigue, illness, anxiety, sorrow, harm, or grief afflicts a Muslim, even the pricking of a thorn, except that Allah expiates some of their sins thereby' (Bukhari and Muslim).

Sabr and Shukr Together

The Prophet said: 'How wonderful is the affair of the believer, for all of it is good. If something good happens, he is grateful and that is good for him. If something bad happens, he is patient and that is good for him' (Muslim). Sabr and shukr (gratitude) together form the complete response of a believer to everything life brings.

title: Shukr — Gratitude to Allah
content:
Definition

Shukr (شكر) means gratitude and thankfulness, expressed through the heart, tongue, and limbs. In Islam, gratitude is not merely a feeling but a comprehensive response to Allah's blessings that manifests in acknowledgment, praise, and using blessings in ways that please the One who granted them.

Quranic Emphasis

Allah says: 'If you are grateful, I will surely increase you' (14:7). This verse establishes a divine principle: gratitude brings increase, while ingratitude brings loss. The Quran also notes: 'And few of My servants are grateful' (34:13), indicating that true gratitude is rare and therefore highly valued by Allah.

Three Dimensions of Gratitude

Scholars describe shukr as having three components. Gratitude of the heart means recognizing that every blessing comes from Allah alone and feeling genuine appreciation. Gratitude of the tongue means praising Allah verbally, saying Alhamdulillah regularly, and speaking about His blessings. Gratitude of the limbs means using every blessing in obedience to Allah: using healthy eyes to read the Quran, using wealth to help others, and using time in productive worship.

The Grateful Servant

When Aisha asked the Prophet why he prayed at night until his feet swelled when Allah had already forgiven all his sins, he replied: 'Should I not be a grateful servant?' (Bukhari). This shows that gratitude is expressed through increased worship, not complacency. The more one receives, the more one should give thanks through action.

title: Ikhlas — Sincerity of Intention
content:
Definition

Ikhlas (إخلاص) means sincerity and purity of intention in all acts of worship and obedience. It means performing every deed solely for the sake of Allah, free from any desire for worldly recognition, praise, or personal gain. Ikhlas is the soul of every good deed; without it, even the greatest acts of worship become worthless.

The Foundation of Acceptance

The Prophet said: 'Actions are judged by their intentions, and every person will get what they intended' (Bukhari and Muslim). This hadith, considered by scholars to be one-third of the entire religion, establishes that Allah does not accept any deed unless it is done sincerely for His sake. A person who gives charity to be praised by people receives that praise but gets no reward from Allah.

Threats to Ikhlas

The greatest enemy of ikhlas is riya (showing off). The Prophet called riya 'the minor shirk' and said it was the thing he feared most for his ummah (Ahmad). Riya can be subtle: praying more beautifully when someone is watching, speaking about one's charitable donations, or seeking positions of religious authority for status rather than service. Scholars also warn against ujb (self-admiration), where a person becomes impressed with their own worship.

Cultivating Sincerity

Ibn al-Qayyim recommended several practices for strengthening ikhlas: performing secret acts of worship that only Allah knows about, asking Allah regularly for sincerity, reflecting on the fleeting nature of worldly praise, and remembering that on the Day of Judgment only Allah's acceptance matters. Sufyan al-Thawri said: 'I never treated anything more difficult than my intention, because it keeps changing on me.'

title: Khushu — Humility and Focus in Prayer
content:
Definition

Khushu (خشوع) refers to humility, attentiveness, and devotion of the heart during worship, particularly in salah (prayer). It is the state where the heart is fully present before Allah, the body is still, and the mind is focused on the meaning of what is being recited and the greatness of the One being worshipped.

Quranic Praise

Allah says: 'Successful indeed are the believers, those who have khushu in their prayer' (23:1-2). The fact that khushu is the first quality mentioned after faith itself shows its critical importance. Allah also says: 'And seek help through patience and prayer, and indeed it is difficult except for those who have khushu' (2:45).

How to Achieve Khushu

Scholars offer practical guidance for developing khushu in prayer. Before prayer: make proper wudu with mindfulness, pray on time, remove distractions from the prayer area, and think about who you are about to stand before. During prayer: focus on the meanings of al-Fatihah and whatever surah follows, pause between movements, do not rush, visualize the Kaaba or imagine you can see Allah, and remember that this prayer could be your last.

Signs of Khushu

A person with khushu finds comfort and peace in prayer rather than viewing it as a burden. They are unhurried in their movements, their eyes do not wander, and they emerge from prayer feeling refreshed and spiritually nourished. The Prophet's companions reported that when he prayed, a sound like a boiling pot could be heard from his chest due to his weeping (Abu Dawud). This was the depth of his khushu.

title: Birr — Righteousness and Good Conduct
content:
Definition

Birr (بر) is a comprehensive term meaning righteousness, piety, kindness, and good conduct. It encompasses all forms of goodness in Islam, from acts of worship to ethical behavior toward others. The Quran uses birr to describe the complete, well-rounded righteousness that Allah loves.

The Verse of Birr

One of the most comprehensive verses in the Quran defines birr: 'Righteousness is not that you turn your faces toward the east or the west, but righteousness is in one who believes in Allah, the Last Day, the Angels, the Books, and the Prophets, and gives wealth in spite of love for it to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveler, those who ask, and for freeing slaves, and who establishes prayer and gives zakat, and those who fulfill their promise when they make one, and who are patient in poverty, hardship, and during battle. Those are the ones who have been true, and it is those who are the righteous' (2:177).

Birr al-Walidayn

One of the most important applications of birr is toward parents (birr al-walidayn). Allah pairs obedience to parents with worship of Him in several verses. The Prophet said that the pleasure of Allah is in the pleasure of parents, and the displeasure of Allah is in the displeasure of parents (Tirmidhi). This duty continues even after parents pass away through dua, charity on their behalf, and maintaining their friendships.

Birr in Social Relations

The Prophet said: 'Birr is good character, and sin is what disturbs your soul and you dislike people finding out about' (Muslim). This narration links birr directly to akhlaq (character) and provides an internal compass: if an action would embarrass you before Allah or righteous people, it is not from birr.

title: Istiqamah — Steadfastness on the Straight Path
content:
Definition

Istiqamah (استقامة) means steadfastness, consistency, and firmness upon the straight path of Islam. It refers to maintaining a constant state of obedience to Allah, neither swerving toward excess nor falling into deficiency. The Prophet described istiqamah as the most comprehensive advice he could give, saying: 'Say I believe in Allah, then be steadfast' (Muslim).

Quranic Command

Allah commanded His Prophet: 'So remain on a right course as you have been commanded, you and those who have turned back with you, and do not transgress' (11:112). The Prophet said this verse and those like it caused his hair to turn gray, indicating the weight and difficulty of maintaining istiqamah. Umar ibn al-Khattab explained istiqamah as 'remaining firm on the commandments and prohibitions without dodging like a fox.'

Istiqamah vs. Perfection

Istiqamah does not mean perfection or never sinning. The Prophet said: 'Be steadfast, but you will not be able to perfectly, so know that the best of your deeds is the prayer' (Ahmad). Human beings will falter, but istiqamah means always returning to the path after a slip. It means consistency in worship even when motivation is low, maintaining good character even under pressure, and never abandoning the fundamentals of faith regardless of circumstances.

Practical Istiqamah

Scholars advise several practices for maintaining istiqamah: establishing a daily routine of worship that you never abandon, keeping company with righteous people, continuously seeking knowledge, making frequent tawbah (repentance), and asking Allah for firmness. The dua the Prophet made most frequently was: 'O Turner of hearts, keep my heart firm upon Your religion' (Tirmidhi).

title: Al-Quds (Jerusalem) — The Blessed Land
content:
Historical Significance

Al-Quds (القدس), known in English as Jerusalem, holds a place of immense importance in Islam as the third holiest city after Makkah and Madinah. The city's sanctity predates Islam, as it was a center of prophetic activity for thousands of years. Prophets Ibrahim, Dawud, Sulayman, Zakariyya, Yahya, and Isa all lived and preached there. The land surrounding it is described in the Quran as 'blessed' (17:1, 21:71, 21:81).

Isra and Mi'raj

Al-Quds is the site of one of the most significant events in the Prophet's life. During the Night Journey (Isra), the Prophet was transported from Masjid al-Haram in Makkah to Masjid al-Aqsa in Jerusalem, where he led all the previous prophets in prayer. From there, he ascended through the seven heavens (Mi'raj). This event is referenced in Surah al-Isra: 'Glory be to the One who took His servant by night from al-Masjid al-Haram to al-Masjid al-Aqsa, whose surroundings We have blessed' (17:1).

First Qiblah

For approximately sixteen months after the Hijrah to Madinah, Muslims prayed facing Jerusalem. The change of qiblah to Makkah came through a Quranic revelation (2:144), but this period cemented Jerusalem's sacred status in the Muslim consciousness. The city remained a destination of pilgrimage and scholarship throughout Islamic history.

Islamic Jerusalem Through the Ages

When Umar ibn al-Khattab accepted Jerusalem's surrender in 637 CE, he personally cleaned the site of al-Aqsa and guaranteed the safety of all inhabitants regardless of faith. The Umayyad caliph Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan built the Dome of the Rock in 691 CE. Under Muslim rule, Jerusalem was generally a city of coexistence where Christians, Jews, and Muslims lived together. Salahuddin al-Ayyubi's reconquest in 1187 CE was noted for its mercy and justice compared to the Crusader conquest of 1099.

title: Damascus in Islamic History
content:
The Oldest Inhabited City

Damascus (دمشق), the capital of modern Syria, is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. It became a major center of Islamic civilization after its conquest in 634 CE by the Muslim army under Khalid ibn al-Walid and Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah. The city's strategic location on trade routes between the Mediterranean and the East made it a natural hub for culture, commerce, and scholarship.

Capital of the Umayyad Caliphate

In 661 CE, Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan established Damascus as the capital of the Umayyad Caliphate, inaugurating a golden age for the city. Under Umayyad rule, the Great Mosque of Damascus (the Umayyad Mosque) was built by Caliph al-Walid I between 705 and 715 CE. This architectural masterpiece became a model for mosque design across the Muslim world. The mosque houses what is believed to be the head of Prophet Yahya (John the Baptist).

Center of Learning

Damascus became one of the greatest centers of Islamic learning. Scholars from across the Muslim world traveled there to study and teach. Ibn Taymiyyah taught and was imprisoned in the Damascus Citadel. Ibn al-Qayyim al-Jawziyyah, his most famous student, also lived and worked there. Al-Nawawi, the great Shafi'i scholar and author of Riyad as-Salihin and his famous Forty Hadith collection, was based in the Damascus region.

Legacy

The Umayyad Mosque remains a functioning place of worship and a destination for visitors from around the world. Damascus's Islamic legacy is visible in its Old City, its numerous madrasas and mausoleums, and its continuing role as a center of traditional Islamic education. The city's scholars played key roles in preserving hadith literature, developing legal theory, and producing some of the most widely-read works in Islamic history.

title: Baghdad — Capital of the Golden Age
content:
Founding and Rise

Baghdad (بغداد) was founded in 762 CE by the Abbasid Caliph al-Mansur as the new capital of the Abbasid Caliphate. Originally called Madinat al-Salam (City of Peace), it was designed as a round city with the caliph's palace at its center. Within decades, Baghdad grew to become the largest and most prosperous city in the world, with a population exceeding one million at its peak.

The Islamic Golden Age

Baghdad was the intellectual capital of the world from the 8th to the 13th century. The Bayt al-Hikmah (House of Wisdom), established under Caliph Harun al-Rashid and expanded by al-Ma'mun, was a major center for translating Greek, Persian, and Indian works into Arabic. Scholars of all backgrounds worked together on mathematics, astronomy, medicine, philosophy, and geography. Al-Khwarizmi developed algebra here. Al-Kindi pioneered Islamic philosophy. The world's first hospitals, libraries, and observatories were established.

Great Scholars of Baghdad

Baghdad produced or hosted many of Islam's greatest minds. Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal was born, taught, and was imprisoned here for his principled stance during the Mihna (inquisition). Imam Abu Hanifah, though he predated the city's founding, has his famous mosque and tomb there. Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi wrote the monumental history of Baghdad's scholars. Abdul-Qadir al-Jilani, founder of the Qadiriyyah order, taught and is buried in Baghdad.

The Mongol Destruction

In 1258 CE, Hulagu Khan's Mongol army sacked Baghdad, killing hundreds of thousands and destroying the House of Wisdom. The Tigris is said to have run black with the ink of the books thrown into it. This event marked the end of the Abbasid Caliphate in Baghdad and a devastating blow to Islamic civilization, though scholarship continued in other centers like Cairo, Damascus, and the newly rising Ottoman cities.

title: Cordoba — Jewel of Al-Andalus
content:
The Umayyad Emirate

Cordoba (قرطبة) in present-day Spain was the capital of Muslim Al-Andalus and one of the most advanced cities in medieval Europe. After the Abbasid revolution overthrew the Umayyads in the East, Abd al-Rahman I (the Falcon of the Quraysh) escaped to Iberia and established the Umayyad Emirate of Cordoba in 756 CE. His descendants would transform the city into a center of civilization that rivaled Baghdad and Constantinople.

A City of Light

By the 10th century, Cordoba had paved streets, street lighting, running water, public baths, hospitals, and a population of around 500,000, making it one of the largest cities in Europe. The city had 70 libraries, with the caliph's personal library containing an estimated 400,000 volumes at a time when the largest library in Christian Europe held a few hundred. Education was nearly universal, and literacy rates far exceeded those elsewhere in Europe.

The Great Mosque

The Great Mosque of Cordoba (La Mezquita), begun in 784 CE by Abd al-Rahman I, is one of the architectural wonders of the world. Its forest of red and white horseshoe arches, supported by over 850 columns, creates a mesmerizing visual effect. It was expanded multiple times over two centuries. After the Christian conquest, a cathedral was built inside the mosque, but the Islamic architecture remains largely intact and draws millions of visitors annually.

Intellectual Legacy

Cordoba produced scholars whose works influenced both the Muslim world and Christian Europe. Ibn Rushd (Averroes) wrote commentaries on Aristotle that shaped European philosophy. Ibn Hazm wrote extensively on comparative religion and Islamic jurisprudence. Abbas ibn Firnas attempted controlled flight centuries before the Wright brothers. Maslama al-Majriti advanced mathematics and astronomy. The translation movement from Arabic to Latin in Cordoba and Toledo transmitted Muslim scholarship to the rest of Europe, helping spark the Renaissance.

title: Istanbul — Where East Meets West
content:
The Conquest of Constantinople

Istanbul (إسطنبول), originally Constantinople, was conquered by Sultan Mehmed II (Mehmed the Conqueror) on May 29, 1453 CE. The Prophet Muhammad had prophesied this conquest: 'You will certainly conquer Constantinople. What a wonderful army that army will be, and what a wonderful commander its commander will be' (Ahmad). Mehmed was 21 years old when he fulfilled this prophecy, ending the Byzantine Empire after over a millennium.

Capital of the Ottoman Caliphate

Istanbul served as the capital of the Ottoman Empire for nearly five centuries (1453-1922). Under Ottoman rule, the city became the seat of the Caliphate and one of the greatest Islamic capitals in history. The Ottomans transformed the Hagia Sophia into a mosque and built magnificent new mosques, including the Suleymaniye, the Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed), and the Fatih Mosque. The city's skyline of domes and minarets remains iconic.

Center of Learning and Culture

Istanbul housed some of the greatest Islamic educational institutions. Madrasas attached to major mosques trained scholars, judges, and administrators. The Suleymaniye complex included a medical school, a hospital, a library, and a soup kitchen. Calligraphers, miniature painters, architects, and poets flourished under Ottoman patronage. Mimar Sinan, the greatest Ottoman architect, designed over 300 structures in and around the city.

Modern Istanbul

Today Istanbul straddles Europe and Asia across the Bosphorus strait, symbolizing the bridge between civilizations. Its historical mosques, the Grand Bazaar, and the manuscript collections in the Topkapi Palace and Suleymaniye Library preserve a vast Islamic heritage. The city remains a hub for Islamic scholarship, publishing, and cultural production, with a population exceeding 15 million.

title: Timbuktu — City of 333 Saints
content:
An African Center of Learning

Timbuktu (تمبكتو), located in present-day Mali at the southern edge of the Sahara Desert, was one of the most important centers of Islamic scholarship in Africa. Founded around the 12th century, it grew into a wealthy trading city and a legendary seat of learning. At its height in the 15th and 16th centuries, Timbuktu was home to three great mosques and one of the world's first universities.

The University of Sankore

The Sankore Mosque and its associated university attracted scholars from across the Muslim world. At its peak, the university had approximately 25,000 students studying Islamic sciences, mathematics, astronomy, medicine, law, and history. The curriculum was comparable to the best institutions in the Muslim world. Students progressed through levels of study and received ijazahs (certifications) from their teachers, a system that influenced the modern university degree.

The Manuscript Tradition

Timbuktu is famous for its manuscripts. Hundreds of thousands of manuscripts covering every branch of Islamic knowledge were produced, copied, and collected here. Families guarded their manuscript collections for generations. Many of these manuscripts survive today and are being preserved and digitized. They cover theology, law, medicine, astronomy, mathematics, poetry, and history, demonstrating the depth of Islamic scholarship in West Africa.

Legacy

Timbuktu challenges the narrative that sub-Saharan Africa lacked intellectual traditions. Its scholars, including Ahmad Baba al-Timbukti (a prolific author and jurist who wrote over 60 works), contributed significantly to Islamic civilization. The city's three great mosques, Djinguereber, Sankore, and Sidi Yahia, are UNESCO World Heritage Sites. Timbuktu remains a symbol of the global reach of Islamic knowledge and the rich tradition of African Muslim scholarship.

title: Cairo — The City Victorious
content:
Founding and Name

Cairo (القاهرة, al-Qahirah, meaning 'the Victorious') was founded in 969 CE by the Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli as a royal enclave near the older city of Fustat. The city quickly grew to become one of the largest and most important cities in the Islamic world. Its strategic location on the Nile, near the head of the Delta, made it a natural center for trade, culture, and governance.

Al-Azhar University

The Fatimids established al-Azhar Mosque in 970 CE, and it soon developed into al-Azhar University, the oldest continuously operating degree-granting university in the world. For over a thousand years, al-Azhar has been the preeminent institution of Sunni Islamic learning. Scholars trained at al-Azhar have shaped Islamic thought across the globe. The university teaches all four madhabs and has been a center for moderation and mainstream Sunni scholarship.

The Mamluk Era

Under the Mamluks (1250-1517 CE), Cairo reached its architectural and cultural zenith. The Mamluks built hundreds of mosques, madrasas, hospitals, and caravanserais, many of which still stand. The Sultan Hasan Mosque-Madrasa complex (1356-1363) is considered one of the finest examples of Islamic architecture. After the Mongol destruction of Baghdad in 1258, Cairo became the undisputed cultural capital of the Islamic world, hosting scholars, poets, and scientists who fled the devastation in the east.

Scholarly Legacy

Cairo has been home to some of the greatest scholars in Islamic history. Ibn Khaldun wrote his Muqaddimah there. Al-Suyuti produced over 500 works while based in Cairo. Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani, the author of Fath al-Bari (the greatest commentary on Sahih Bukhari), served as the chief judge of Egypt. The city's manuscript collections, housed in institutions like Dar al-Kutub, are among the richest in the world.

title: The Hijrah — Migration to Madinah
content:
Background

The Hijrah (هجرة) refers to the Prophet Muhammad's migration from Makkah to Madinah in 622 CE, one of the most pivotal events in Islamic history. After thirteen years of preaching in Makkah, the Muslims faced severe persecution from the Quraysh. The Prophet had sent groups of Muslims to Abyssinia earlier, but the Hijrah to Madinah represented the establishment of the first Muslim community-state. The event was so significant that Umar ibn al-Khattab later chose it as the starting point for the Islamic calendar.

The Journey

The Quraysh plotted to assassinate the Prophet, assigning young men from each clan to strike simultaneously so that no single clan could be blamed. Ali ibn Abi Talib slept in the Prophet's bed as a decoy while the Prophet and Abu Bakr slipped away under cover of night. They hid in the Cave of Thawr for three days while the Quraysh searched for them. Allah sent a spider to spin a web and a dove to nest at the cave's entrance. Abu Bakr was anxious, but the Prophet reassured him: 'Do not grieve, indeed Allah is with us' (9:40).

Arrival in Madinah

The people of Madinah, the Ansar (Helpers), greeted the Prophet with songs and celebration. The Prophet established brotherhood (mu'akhah) between the Muhajirin (migrants from Makkah) and the Ansar, pairing each migrant family with a local family who shared their wealth and homes. This unprecedented act of solidarity laid the foundation for the Muslim community in Madinah.

The Constitution of Madinah

Shortly after arriving, the Prophet drafted the Constitution of Madinah (Sahifat al-Madinah), a document that established a multi-religious, multi-tribal polity. It defined the rights and responsibilities of Muslims, Jews, and other groups living in Madinah, establishing principles of mutual defense, religious freedom, and collective governance. Many scholars consider it one of the earliest written constitutions in history.

title: The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah
content:
Context

In the sixth year after the Hijrah (628 CE), the Prophet Muhammad set out for Makkah with approximately 1,400 companions to perform Umrah (the lesser pilgrimage). They were not armed for war, wearing ihram garments and bringing sacrificial animals to demonstrate their peaceful intentions. The Quraysh, however, blocked their entry at Hudaybiyyah, on the outskirts of Makkah.

Negotiations and the Treaty

After extensive negotiations, a treaty was signed between the Muslims and the Quraysh. The terms appeared unfavorable to the Muslims: they would return to Madinah without performing Umrah that year, would come back the following year for only three days, any Quraysh member who fled to Madinah would be returned, but any Muslim who fled to Makkah would not be returned. Many companions were deeply upset, particularly Umar ibn al-Khattab, who questioned why Muslims should accept such terms.

A Clear Victory

Allah revealed: 'Indeed, We have granted you a clear victory' (48:1), describing the treaty as a triumph. The wisdom became apparent over time. The ten-year truce allowed the Prophet to send letters to world leaders inviting them to Islam. It allowed free movement and interaction between Muslims and non-Muslims, leading to more conversions in two years than in the previous nineteen combined. The Quraysh's violation of the treaty two years later led directly to the peaceful liberation of Makkah.

Lessons

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah teaches several principles. Strategic patience can achieve more than confrontation. Short-term setbacks may lead to long-term gains. The Prophet's willingness to negotiate and compromise on non-essential matters while holding firm on principles demonstrated masterful leadership. The companions' trust in the Prophet, even when they could not see the wisdom, exemplified the relationship between a leader and their community.

title: The Liberation of Makkah (Fath Makkah)
content:
Background

The Liberation of Makkah (فتح مكة) took place in Ramadan of the 8th year after Hijrah (630 CE). After the Quraysh violated the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah by supporting an attack on the Banu Khuza'ah, allies of the Muslims, the Prophet mobilized an army of 10,000, the largest Muslim force assembled to that point. The march was conducted with such secrecy that the Quraysh were unaware until the army was at the outskirts of Makkah.

The Peaceful Entry

The Prophet gave strict orders to avoid bloodshed. He instructed: 'Whoever enters the house of Abu Sufyan is safe, whoever closes his door is safe, and whoever enters the Masjid al-Haram is safe.' Abu Sufyan, the longtime leader of Quraysh opposition, embraced Islam when he witnessed the magnitude of the Muslim force. Makkah was taken with virtually no resistance; only a few skirmishes occurred at one entry point. This stands in stark contrast to how cities were typically conquered in the ancient world.

Forgiveness and Mercy

After entering Makkah, the Prophet went to the Ka'bah and destroyed the 360 idols, reciting: 'Truth has come and falsehood has departed. Indeed falsehood is bound to depart' (17:81). He then addressed the Quraysh, who had persecuted Muslims for two decades, tortured and killed his companions, and driven him from his home. He asked: 'What do you think I will do with you?' They replied: 'You are a noble brother, son of a noble brother.' He said: 'Go, you are free.' This act of clemency won the hearts of the Makkans, and most embraced Islam.

Significance

The liberation of Makkah marked the decisive end of organized pagan resistance in Arabia. It demonstrated that Islam's ultimate goal was not military domination but the removal of barriers to faith. The Prophet's mercy toward his former enemies remains one of the most powerful examples of forgiveness in human history. The event fulfilled the Quranic prophecy and established Makkah as the spiritual center of the Muslim world for all time.

title: The Battle of Badr
content:
Background

The Battle of Badr (غزوة بدر), fought on 17 Ramadan, 2 AH (March 624 CE), was the first major military engagement between the Muslims and the Quraysh. The Muslims, numbering approximately 313 men with minimal equipment (two horses and seventy camels), faced a Quraysh army of about 1,000 well-armed soldiers with 100 horses and 700 camels. The battle took place at the wells of Badr, about 130 kilometers southwest of Madinah.

The Battle

The Prophet consulted his companions before engaging. Al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad declared: 'We will not say to you what the people of Musa said. Go, you and your Lord, and fight. We will remain here. Rather, go, you and your Lord, and fight. We will fight alongside you.' Sa'd ibn Mu'adh of the Ansar pledged similar support. The Prophet positioned his forces at the wells, and the battle began with individual combat. Hamzah, Ali, and Ubaydah ibn al-Harith defeated the three Quraysh champions. The general battle followed, and despite being heavily outnumbered, the Muslims achieved a decisive victory.

Divine Assistance

The Quran describes divine intervention at Badr: 'Remember when you asked help of your Lord, and He answered you: I will reinforce you with a thousand angels, following one another' (8:9). The Muslims attributed their victory to Allah's help, understanding that numbers and equipment alone do not determine outcomes. The Quran calls the day of Badr 'Yawm al-Furqan' (the Day of Criterion), as it distinguished truth from falsehood.

Impact

Badr transformed the Muslim community from a persecuted minority into a recognized force in Arabia. Seventy Quraysh leaders were killed, including Abu Jahl, and seventy were captured. The Prophet treated prisoners with unprecedented dignity, assigning each prisoner to a companion's household and instructing that they be fed before their captors ate. Literate prisoners could earn their freedom by teaching ten Muslims to read and write. The victory at Badr boosted Muslim morale, weakened the Quraysh's prestige, and proved that the nascent Islamic state could defend itself.

title: The Battle of Uhud
content:
Background

The Battle of Uhud (غزوة أحد) took place on 7 Shawwal, 3 AH (March 625 CE), one year after Badr. The Quraysh, seeking revenge, assembled an army of 3,000 men under Abu Sufyan. The Prophet consulted his companions, and the majority favored meeting the enemy outside Madinah. The Muslim force numbered approximately 1,000, but Abdullah ibn Ubayy (the chief of the hypocrites) withdrew with 300 men before the battle, leaving 700 Muslims to face the Quraysh at Mount Uhud, north of Madinah.

The Battle and the Turning Point

The Prophet stationed fifty archers on a hill with strict orders not to leave their positions under any circumstances. The initial Muslim charge was devastating, and the Quraysh began to retreat. However, when the archers saw the enemy fleeing and the spoils of war being collected, most abandoned their posts against the Prophet's explicit command. Khalid ibn al-Walid, then fighting for the Quraysh, exploited this gap, leading his cavalry around the hill and attacking the Muslims from behind.

Aftermath

The reversal was severe. Seventy Muslims were martyred, including Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib, the Prophet's uncle and one of Islam's greatest warriors. The Prophet himself was wounded, losing a tooth and suffering cuts to his face. False reports of the Prophet's death spread across the battlefield, causing further confusion. Despite the setback, the Quraysh did not pursue their advantage and withdrew to Makkah without pressing into Madinah.

Lessons

The Quran devoted over sixty verses (3:121-179) to analyzing Uhud. The key lessons include: obedience to leadership is paramount; love of worldly gain (the spoils) can lead to disaster; setbacks test and purify the community; and even the Prophet's companions are not immune to error. Allah says: 'If a wound has afflicted you, a similar wound has already afflicted the other people. We alternate these days of victory and defeat among people' (3:140). Uhud taught the Muslims that victory comes only from Allah and requires complete discipline and obedience.

title: Waqf — Islamic Endowment
content:
Definition

Waqf (وقف) is an Islamic endowment where a person dedicates a physical asset (land, building, or other property) for a charitable or religious purpose in perpetuity. Once designated as waqf, the property cannot be sold, inherited, or donated. Only its revenue or usufruct benefits the designated cause. The concept is unique to Islamic civilization and was one of the primary engines of social welfare, education, and infrastructure in the Muslim world for over a millennium.

Legal Basis

The concept originates from a hadith where the Prophet advised Umar ibn al-Khattab regarding land he had acquired in Khaybar: 'If you wish, you may hold the property and give its produce as charity.' Umar did so, stipulating that the land itself could never be sold, inherited, or given away, while its produce would go to the poor, relatives, slaves seeking freedom, travelers, and guests (Bukhari and Muslim). This became the foundational model for all waqf endowments.

Historical Impact

Waqf endowments funded mosques, schools, hospitals, orphanages, soup kitchens, water fountains, bridges, roads, libraries, and even animal shelters across the Muslim world. By the Ottoman period, an estimated one-third to one-half of all agricultural land in the empire was waqf property. Major institutions like al-Azhar University, the Suleymaniye complex in Istanbul, and hundreds of hospitals and libraries were all waqf-funded, operating for centuries without government support.

Modern Revival

Colonial-era policies dismantled many waqf systems, nationalizing properties and breaking the chain of charitable endowments. Today, several Muslim-majority countries are working to revive the waqf system. Modern waqf can include cash waqf, corporate shares, and intellectual property. Malaysia, Turkey, and the Gulf states have established waqf boards to manage new endowments, recognizing the institution's potential to address contemporary social needs through a self-sustaining charitable model.

title: Bayt al-Mal — The Public Treasury
content:
Definition

Bayt al-Mal (بيت المال, literally 'House of Wealth') was the public treasury in Islamic governance, responsible for collecting and distributing state revenues. It served as the financial institution of the Islamic state, managing zakat, kharaj (land tax), jizya (protection tax from non-Muslims), and other revenues. The concept established that public wealth belongs to the community, not the ruler, and must be spent for the common good.

Establishment Under Abu Bakr and Umar

While the Prophet managed public finances directly during his lifetime, the institution was formalized under Abu Bakr and significantly expanded under Umar ibn al-Khattab. Umar established a comprehensive registry (diwan) that documented every citizen's entitlement from the treasury. He allocated stipends based on early contribution to Islam, military service, and need. Umar famously said: 'If a mule stumbles in Iraq, I fear that Allah will ask me why I did not pave the road for it.'

Functions

The Bayt al-Mal served several critical functions. It collected and distributed zakat to its eight designated categories. It funded military defense. It provided welfare to orphans, widows, the disabled, the elderly, and anyone unable to work. It paid the salaries of judges, governors, teachers, and public servants. It funded public infrastructure: roads, bridges, canals, and public buildings. It maintained emergency reserves for famines, floods, and other disasters.

Principles

Several principles governed the Bayt al-Mal. The ruler was a trustee, not an owner, of public funds. Spending had to serve the public interest. Transparency and accountability were required. Waste and extravagance were prohibited. Umar refused to use any treasury funds for personal expenses beyond a modest stipend. When Ali ibn Abi Talib became caliph, he distributed the entire treasury regularly, refusing to accumulate wealth. These principles of public finance were practiced in the Muslim world centuries before they appeared in Western governance.

title: Shura — Consultation in Islam
content:
Definition

Shura (شورى) means consultation or mutual counsel. It is a fundamental principle of Islamic governance and decision-making. Allah commands the Prophet: 'And consult them in the matter' (3:159) and praises the believers as 'those who conduct their affairs by mutual consultation' (42:38). Shura ensures that decisions affecting the community are not made unilaterally but incorporate the wisdom and perspectives of qualified members.

The Prophet's Example

The Prophet practiced shura consistently, even though he received divine revelation. Before the Battle of Badr, he consulted his companions about whether to engage the enemy. Before the Battle of Uhud, he deferred to the majority opinion to fight outside Madinah, even though his own preference was to defend from within the city. Before the Battle of the Trench, he adopted Salman al-Farsi's suggestion to dig a defensive trench, a strategy unknown in Arabia. These examples show that shura was not ceremonial but genuinely influenced decisions.

Scope and Limits

Scholars clarify that shura applies to worldly affairs, administrative decisions, and matters where no clear text from the Quran or Sunnah exists. It does not apply to established religious rulings. Shura does not necessarily mean majority rule in all cases; the leader may override the majority if they have superior knowledge or insight, but they bear responsibility for the decision. The Quran says 'consult them' and then 'when you have decided, rely on Allah' (3:159), indicating that consultation informs but does not always bind the decision-maker.

Historical Practice

The Rashidun caliphs exemplified shura. Abu Bakr consulted senior companions on major decisions. Umar established a formal consultative council (majlis al-shura) and appointed a six-member committee to choose his successor. This committee selected Uthman after extensive deliberation. Throughout Islamic history, rulers who abandoned shura were criticized by scholars, and those who practiced it were praised. The principle remains central to discussions of Islamic governance today.

title: Hisbah — Enjoining Good and Forbidding Evil
content:
Definition

Hisbah (حسبة) is the Islamic institution responsible for commanding right and forbidding wrong (al-amr bil-ma'ruf wal-nahy an al-munkar). As a personal duty, every Muslim is obligated to promote good and oppose evil according to their ability. As an institution, the hisbah was a government body that ensured public morality, market integrity, consumer protection, and urban order in Islamic cities.

Quranic and Prophetic Basis

Allah says: 'Let there arise from you a group inviting to good, enjoining what is right, and forbidding what is wrong. Those are the successful ones' (3:104). The Prophet said: 'Whoever among you sees an evil, let him change it with his hand. If he cannot, then with his tongue. If he cannot, then with his heart, and that is the weakest of faith' (Muslim). These texts establish both the individual obligation and the communal responsibility for maintaining moral and social order.

The Muhtasib

The muhtasib was the official responsible for hisbah. Their duties included inspecting markets for fraud, checking weights and measures, ensuring food safety and quality, preventing price gouging, maintaining cleanliness of public spaces, addressing noise complaints, ensuring buildings did not encroach on roads, and upholding public decorum. The muhtasib had the authority to inspect, fine, and punish violators. This role combined what modern governments split among market regulators, health inspectors, building inspectors, and consumer protection agencies.

Historical Significance

The hisbah system was remarkably sophisticated. Al-Mawardi, al-Ghazali, and Ibn Taymiyyah all wrote detailed treatises on its principles and procedures. The institution ensured that Islamic markets were among the most regulated and fair in the medieval world. Visiting European merchants noted the honesty and order of Muslim markets, and many hisbah principles influenced later European trade regulations. The concept remains relevant today as Muslim-majority countries explore how to integrate hisbah principles into modern regulatory frameworks.

title: Ilm al-Kalam — Islamic Theology
content:
Definition

Ilm al-Kalam (علم الكلام), literally 'the science of discourse,' is Islamic systematic theology. It is the discipline that addresses foundational questions of faith using rational arguments: the existence of God, the nature of divine attributes, free will versus predestination, the created or uncreated nature of the Quran, and the relationship between reason and revelation. Kalam developed in response to both internal theological debates and external challenges from Greek philosophy, Christianity, and other traditions.

Historical Development

The earliest theological debates emerged in the first Islamic century over questions of faith versus works (the Murji'ah vs. Khawarij debate), divine attributes (the Mu'tazilah vs. traditionalists), and free will versus predestination (Qadariyyah vs. Jabriyyah). The Mu'tazilah, who emphasized rational theology, became dominant under the Abbasid caliphs al-Ma'mun, al-Mu'tasim, and al-Wathiq, imposing the doctrine that the Quran was created through the Mihna (inquisition). Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal's principled resistance to this doctrine became legendary.

The Ash'ari and Maturidi Schools

Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari (d. 936 CE), originally a Mu'tazili, broke away and developed a middle path that used rational methodology to defend traditional Sunni beliefs. Abu Mansur al-Maturidi (d. 944 CE) independently developed a similar approach in Central Asia. Both schools became the dominant theological frameworks in Sunni Islam. The Ash'ari school predominates in the Shafi'i and Maliki madhabs, while the Maturidi school predominates among Hanafis.

The Athari Position

The Athari (textualist) approach, associated with the Hanbali school and scholars like Ibn Taymiyyah, holds that the early Muslims (Salaf) did not engage in kalam and that theological questions should be answered directly from the texts without philosophical elaboration. Atharis accept the divine attributes as stated in the Quran and Sunnah 'without asking how' (bila kayf). All three positions, Ash'ari, Maturidi, and Athari, are accepted within Ahl us-Sunnah wal-Jama'ah.

title: Usul al-Fiqh — Principles of Islamic Jurisprudence
content:
Definition

Usul al-Fiqh (أصول الفقه) is the science of deriving legal rulings from their sources. If fiqh answers the question 'What is the ruling?', usul al-fiqh answers 'How do we arrive at the ruling?' It provides the methodology, principles, and tools that jurists use to extract laws from the Quran, Sunnah, and other recognized sources. Imam al-Shafi'i is credited with formalizing this discipline in his groundbreaking work, al-Risalah.

Primary Sources

The four agreed-upon sources of Islamic law are the Quran, the Sunnah, Ijma (scholarly consensus), and Qiyas (analogical reasoning). The Quran is the supreme authority, and no ruling can contradict it. The Sunnah explains, details, and supplements the Quran. Ijma represents the collective agreement of qualified scholars on a point of law, providing certainty. Qiyas extends existing rulings to new situations that share the same effective cause (illah).

Supplementary Sources

Beyond the four primary sources, scholars employ additional tools. Istihsan (juristic preference) allows departing from a strict analogy when it leads to hardship. Maslahah mursalah (public interest) permits rulings based on general welfare when no specific text addresses the issue. Urf (custom) recognizes local customs as legally binding when they do not contradict the Shariah. Sadd al-dhara'i (blocking the means) prohibits permissible actions that commonly lead to prohibited outcomes. Different madhabs give different weight to these supplementary sources.

Importance

Usul al-fiqh ensures that Islamic law is derived systematically rather than arbitrarily. It prevents unqualified individuals from issuing rulings based on personal opinion alone. It provides the framework for addressing new issues that the early sources did not explicitly cover, from medical ethics to financial technology. Every qualified mujtahid (independent jurist) must master usul al-fiqh before issuing legal opinions, making it one of the most intellectually demanding Islamic sciences.
